nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving hill streams, forested floodplain pools, and moist subtropical lowlands of the Western Ghats; areas with dense riparian vegetation, submerged leaf litter, and seasonal water-level fluctuations. The trade name 'Kerala' is applied to fish marketed from or associated with Kerala state (southwestern India); some sources suggest collection near Alipurduar, West Bengal, indicating the precise provenance of trade stock is unresolved.
nativeRange.biotope: Western Ghats hill stream / subtropical floodplain

decorPreferences: tight-fitting lid with humid air gap below
dim lighting with surface vegetation (Ceratopteris spp., floating plants)
dense submerged planting and leaf-litter cover
driftwood and rocks for territory delineation
minimal current — still or very slow flow

diet.notes: Obligate piscivore and invertebrate predator in the wild. In captivity most specimens can be transitioned to non-live alternatives: frozen/thawed fish flesh, earthworms, large bloodworms, krill, and shrimp. Juveniles often started on chironomid larvae (bloodworms) and graduate to meatier items. Avoid feeder goldfish (disease vector) and mammalian/avian meats (linked to fatty liver). Feed every 2–3 days; reduce frequency significantly during the cool winter period.

goodWith: robust large catfish (with extreme caution in very large tanks)
conspecific pairs possible only in large, heavily structured setups with close monitoring
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed
fin-nipping or fast-moving species that may stress the snakehead

summary: An undescribed dwarf snakehead (~20 cm) from the Western Ghats trade circuit, sold as 'Kerala Five Stripe.' Cool-tolerant and requiring seasonal temperature swings; an aggressive obligate piscivore needing species-only housing, a tight-fitting lid, and advanced husbandry. All Channidae are banned from US import and interstate transport under the Lacey Act.
careNotes:
Channa sp. 'Kerala Five Stripe' is an undescribed species circulating in the specialist trade under the 'Kerala' locality label; molecular and morphological placement remains unresolved, with probable affinity to the C. gachua complex or C. pulchra group. Adults reach approximately 20 cm — making this a mid-sized dwarf snakehead relative to large Channa species.

A species-only aquarium of at least 200 L with a footprint of 120 cm or longer is strongly recommended. The tank must have a tight-fitting, escape-proof lid; Channa are notorious jumpers and can squeeze through surprisingly small gaps. Leave a humid air gap of several centimetres between the lid and the water surface — these obligate air-breathers must access warm, moist air or they can develop respiratory distress in cold drafts.

Unlike tropical snakeheads, this species is cool-tolerant and requires genuine seasonal temperature variation: allow the tank to cool to 15–18°C in autumn/winter (unheated room temperature in temperate climates is often sufficient) and warm to 23–28°C in spring/summer. Feeding should be reduced to once a week or less during the cool phase; fish that are kept warm year-round may fail to thrive long-term and breeding is unlikely without a proper cool period.

Water chemistry is not especially demanding: pH 6.0–8.0, soft to moderately hard water (gH 2–20). Weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are recommended. Filtration should provide gentle flow — the species inhabits still and slow-moving waters.

Conspecific aggression is high; pairs can be kept in well-structured, large tanks but must be monitored closely. The fish is an ambush predator and will consume any tankmate it can fit in its mouth. Decor should include dense planting, driftwood, and rocks to break lines of sight.

US hobbyists: the entire Channidae family is listed as injurious wildlife under the Lacey Act (18 U.S.C. § 42); import into the US and interstate transport are federally prohibited regardless of state-level possession laws.
breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder; males orally incubate the egg mass after spawning. Breeding has been achieved in captivity in Germany and by European specialists. A cool winter rest period (15–18°C) followed by a gradual spring warm-up appears to trigger spawning readiness. Females tend to be larger and deeper-bodied than males; males may develop intensified blue-black colouration on the anal and dorsal fins during courtship. Spawning typically occurs near the surface among floating vegetation. The male broods the eggs and young fry; fry are free-swimming within 1–2 weeks and can accept small live foods (micro-worms, baby brine shrimp) once released. Remove the female if aggression increases post-spawn.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Channa sp. 'five stripe'
primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference. Provided tank size (≥120 cm), temperature range (seasonal 12–30°C basis), adult size (~20 cm), diet (piscivore adapting to dead foods), escape risk, mouthbrooder breeding, species-only recommendation, and surface air-breathing requirement. Also used fishy.wiki mirror data (temperature 15–28°C, pH 6.0–8.0, gH 2–20 dH, min tank 80 L) to fill numeric water-parameter gaps.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Channa sp. Kerala Five Stripe
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (~20 cm), species-only housing, tight lid requirement, seasonal temperature variation need, dim lighting and surface vegetation preferences, and paternal mouthbrooder breeding details. Page returned 403 at fetch time; data corroborated via search snippet and SeriouslyFish cross-reference.

additional.name: FishBase — Channa gachua (gachua complex reference)
additional.notes: Used as reference for C. gachua complex water-parameter baselines (temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–7.0, native South/Southeast Asian range) given probable affinity of Kerala Five Stripe to this complex. Kerala Five Stripe parameters broadened to reflect its cool-tolerant subtropical character.

additional.name: U.S. Geological Survey — Can aquarium hobbyists still possess snakeheads as pets?
additional.notes: Confirmed Lacey Act federal prohibition: importation and interstate transport of live snakeheads (entire Channidae family) is prohibited under U.S. federal law. Some states may permit intrastate possession but federal transport ban is absolute.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Channa sp. 5-stripe
additional.notes: German import/wholesaler record. Confirmed seasonal temperature range (12–30°C in nature), subtropical character, mouthbrooder reproductive mode, and maximum size of 15–20 cm. Listed origin as north India (Assam / North Bengal area) — consistent with unresolved trade provenance.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder; bred by European specialists. Requires a genuine cool winter rest (15–18°C) to trigger spawning readiness. Males incubate egg mass orally; fry accept micro-worms and baby brine shrimp once free-swimming. Separate female post-spawn if aggression escalates.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: All Channa species are USDA-listed Injurious Wildlife under the Lacey Act; import and interstate transport banned in the US. Undescribed Kerala (Western Ghats, India) species in trade as a small specialist snakehead.
